visualizations.title: Deterministic Executor: Task Scheduling Stepper
visualizations.learning_goal: Understand how a seeded RNG selects the next task to poll, and why time only advances after a full pass through the ready queue.
visualizations.why_it_matters: Distributed system bugs are scheduling events, not code bugs. The deterministic executor lets you replay any interleaving by shipping the seed — turning a flake into a reproducible test.
visualizations.default_state: Three tasks spawned: Task A (ready), Task B (ready), Task C (sleeping, 5ms). Seed = 42. Cycle duration = 1ms. Clock at UNIX_EPOCH.

scenes.caption: Three tasks are spawned. Task A and Task B are immediately ready (no awaits). Task C calls sleep(5ms) and becomes a sleeper. The executor's ready queue holds [A, B] and the alarm queue holds C with deadline at 5ms.
visual_elements: Ready queue: [A, B] (pill nodes, green)
Sleepers queue: [C at t+5ms] (pill node, amber with clock icon)
RNG state: seed=42 (shown as lock icon + number)
Clock: UNIX_EPOCH + 0ms
Executor loop counter: cycle 0

scenes.caption: Cycle 1 begins. The executor drains the ready queue: [A, B]. The RNG shuffles it with seed=42, producing [B, A]. Task B is polled first, returns Pending (waiting on network), and re-registers its waker. Task A is polled next, also returns Pending. Both remain in the running set. The clock advances by cycle_duration (1ms). Now t=1ms. C's deadline (5ms) has not passed — no wake.
system_events: executor loop: drain ready, shuffle with RNG, poll B, poll A, advance_time(1ms)
visual_elements: Ready queue: [] (drained and re-queued by wakers)
Running tasks: [A(waker=TaskAWaker), B(waker=TaskBWaker)] (blue, labelled with their registered waker)
Clock: 1ms
Shuffle visualization: [A,B] → [B,A] with RNG arrow
Cycle counter: 1

scenes.caption: After 4 more cycles, the clock reaches 5ms. Task C's alarm fires: the executor calls C's waker, which queues C back onto the ready queue. The ready queue now holds [C]. The RNG shuffle is a no-op for a single-element queue. Task C is polled and completes.
system_events: poll C → Ready, remove from running
visual_elements: Ready queue: [C] (single element, no shuffle arrow)
Alarm queue: [] (C's alarm popped)
Task C transitions: amber (sleeping) → green (ready) → struck through (complete)
Clock: 5ms
Executor returns: root future complete

scenes.caption: Same task set, seed=1 instead of 42. On cycle 1, the shuffle produces [A, B] instead of [B, A]. Task A is polled first this time. The execution trace diverges. This is the core of seeded exploration: different seeds produce different schedules, exposing different potential interleavings.
visual_elements: Ready queue order: [A, B] (reversed from seed=42)
Execution trace diverges indicator (red dashed line showing split timeline)
Two columns: 'seed=42' vs 'seed=1' showing divergent task poll order

checks: Ready queue is empty only when all tasks are either sleeping or complete
Clock only advances after a full pass through the ready queue
Shuffle with seed=42 is identical every run
Single-element ready queue produces no shuffle (deterministic ordering)

visualizations.title: Supervision Tree: Abort Cascade
visualizations.learning_goal: Understand structured concurrency via weak-reference abort cascades: aborting a parent cancels all descendants, but siblings are unaffected.
visualizations.why_it_matters: The supervisor tree enforces that child tasks are cleaned up when their parent exits — preventing orphaned background work. Unlike Erlang's OTP supervision strategies, this is purely about cancellation propagation, not message passing or restart policies.
visualizations.default_state: Root spawns Child1 and Child2. Child1 spawns Grandchild1a and Grandchild1b. Child2 spawns Grandchild2a. A 3-level tree. No aborts triggered yet.

scenes.caption: A 3-level task hierarchy. Root is the trunk. Child1 and Child2 are siblings branching from Root. Grandchild1a and Grandchild1b are children of Child1. Grandchild2a is a child of Child2. Each node shows its label and current state (Running/Pending/Complete). Weak references are not yet shown.
visual_elements: Tree diagram: Root at top, Child1 and Child2 below, grandchildren below their parents
Node states: Root=Running (green), Child1=Running (green), Child2=Running (green), GC1a=Running, GC1b=Running, GC2a=Running (all green)
Edge labels: parent→child shown as solid lines
Node pills show task label and Arc reference count (e.g., 'Child1 (ref=2)')

scenes.caption: Child1 is aborted. The abort cascades to Grandchild1a and Grandchild1b — both are cancelled. Child2 and Grandchild2a are untouched siblings and continue running. The weak reference mechanics are shown: each child holds a Weak reference to its parent. When Child1 is aborted, Tree::abort() upgrades each Weak<GC> and calls abort() recursively.
system_events: Tree::abort() → mem::take(children) → for each child Weak::upgrade() → abort()
visual_elements: GC1a and GC1b nodes turn red and are struck through
Child2 and GC2a remain green
Animated red dashed edges show cascade path: Child1 → GC1a, Child1 → GC1b
Weak references shown as dotted arrows from children to parent
Refcount on Child1 drops to 0 (node removed from parent's children list on next child() call via retain)

scenes.caption: Root is aborted. All descendants are cancelled: Child1 (and its entire subtree), Child2 (and its entire subtree). Every node in the tree turns red. The executor detects no tasks remain running and exits. This is structured concurrency's guarantee: no orphaned tasks.
visual_elements: All nodes red/struck through
Root shows 'aborted' state
Executor shows 'stall detection bypassed: all tasks complete'
Final state: empty tree with Root node only, labelled 'aborted'

scenes.caption: Grandchild1a completes normally (without being aborted). Its future is dropped and Tree node is dropped. The parent's children list still holds Weak<Tree> pointing to the now-dropped node. The weak reference upgrade returns None on the next abort cascade. Lazy cleanup: when Tree::child() is called next, the list is pruned via retain(weak.strong_count() > 0).
system_events: Weak ref to GC1a still in Child1.children (not yet cleaned)
visual_elements: GC1a node struck through with 'completed (dropped)' label
Child1.children list shown as: [Weak(GC1a)→NONE, Weak(GC1b)→Some]
Highlight on retain() logic: 'removing stale weak refs' animation

checks: Aborting a node cancels all descendants recursively
Sibling subtrees are unaffected by abort of adjacent subtree
Normal completion (drop) of a child does NOT abort siblings
Weak references prevent refcount cycles (parent holds Arc children, child holds Weak parent)

visualizations.title: Blob Header Parser: On-Disk Structure and Crash Recovery
visualizations.learning_it_matters: Every blob on disk starts with an 8-byte header. Understanding this format is prerequisite to reasoning about crash recovery: partial writes leave a blob shorter than 8 bytes, which triggers automatic truncation and re-initialization.
visualizations.default_state: Hex input: 43 57 49 43 00 00 00 03 (b'CWIC' + runtime v0 + blob v3). Display shows parsed fields.

controls.label: Input Bytes
values: FF FF FF FF FF FF FF FF (corrupt all bits)

scenes.caption: A valid 8-byte header. Bytes 0-3 are the ASCII string 'CWIC' (Commonware Is CWIC). Bytes 4-5 are the runtime version as big-endian u16 (0 = current). Bytes 6-7 are the blob version as big-endian u16 (3 = version 3). The hex bytes highlight in color bands: green for magic, blue for runtime version, orange for blob version. A checkmark indicator shows 'Valid'.
visual_elements: Hex grid: 8 byte cells, each showing index, hex value, and ASCII representation where printable
Field bands: bytes 0-3 green (magic), 4-5 blue (runtime ver), 6-7 orange (blob ver)
Parsed values panel: magic='CWIC' (valid), runtime=0, blob=3
Validity indicator: green checkmark

scenes.caption: Input is only 4 bytes (the magic, no version fields). Header::missing(4) returns true because raw_len < 8. This triggers crash recovery: the storage implementation clears the content and writes a fresh header with the latest blob version. The blob is re-initialized to a clean state. This is how the runtime recovers from an unclean shutdown (power loss during a write).
system_events: Blob re-initialized to empty with fresh header
visual_elements: Hex cells: 4 bytes shown, 4 cells greyed out with 'MISSING' label
Validity: amber warning 'TRUNCATED — triggering recovery'
Recovery animation: old content cleared (strikethrough), new header written (green pulse)
Resulting state: blob size = 8 bytes (fresh header only)

scenes.caption: Magic bytes are not b'CWIC'. Header::from() returns HeaderError::InvalidMagic. This is a BlobCorrupt error, NOT a recovery situation — the blob is genuinely corrupt and cannot be opened. The caller must handle this explicitly. Note the difference: truncation triggers automatic recovery; invalid magic requires explicit error handling.
visual_elements: Magic bytes shown in red with 'INVALID' overlay
Error callout: 'BlobCorrupt: expected CWIC (0x434F5749), found FFFFFFFF'
Red X indicator
Recovery path: NOT available (amber 'No automatic recovery for magic mismatch')

scenes.caption: Blob version 3 is not in the requested range [1, 2]. open_versioned returns BlobVersionMismatch { expected: 1..=2, found: 3 }. This enables forward-compatibility: a reader can specify which blob versions it understands, and the open fails explicitly rather than silently using an incompatible version.
visual_elements: Blob version field highlighted in red: 'found: 3'
Version range bar: [1====2] with 3 shown outside the range in red
Error panel: 'BlobVersionMismatch — blob written by newer version, reader only supports 1..=2'

checks: raw_len < 8 triggers Header::missing → automatic recovery (truncate and re-init)
Magic != b'CWIC' returns HeaderError::InvalidMagic → BlobCorrupt (no recovery)
Blob version outside requested RangeInclusive<u16> returns BlobVersionMismatch with expected and found values
The header is always 8 bytes regardless of logical blob content size

visualizations.title: Network Partition Simulator: Topology, Links, and Message Routing
visualizations.learning_goal: Understand how the deterministic network simulates bind/dial/accept with in-memory channels, assigns ephemeral ports sequentially, and supports configurable link failure rates.
visualizations.why_it_matters: Testing p2p protocols requires simulating network partitions, latency, and message loss. The deterministic network does this without any host network stack involvement — every message is a memcpy across BytesMut buffers.
visualizations.default_state: Four nodes in a mesh topology. All links have latency=10ms, jitter=2ms, drop_rate=0%. Port assignment starts at 32768.

scenes.caption: Three dial operations happen in sequence. Each dial assigns the next ephemeral port from the sequential counter starting at 32768. Port 32768 → first dial, 32769 → second dial, 32770 → third dial. The deterministic port assignment means the same dial sequence always produces the same port assignments — important for reproducible testing.
visual_elements: Port counter: starts at 32768, increments
Three 'dial' animations: Node A → ephemeral:32768, Node B → ephemeral:32769, Node C → ephemeral:32770
In-memory channel shown as BytesMut buffer between dialer and listener
No host ports used (no 0.0.0.0:0 or similar)

scenes.caption: A gossip message is sent from Node 0 to Node 3 via the mesh. The message traverses two hops: 0→2→3. Each hop applies the link's latency and drop rate. With 0% drop, the message arrives after 20ms of simulated time (2 hops × 10ms). The deterministic clock advances 20ms. Drop rate is applied per-hop: each link independently decides whether to deliver or drop.
visual_elements: Animated message packet traveling along edges
Hop counter: [0/2 hops]
Clock showing +10ms per hop
Packet shows: msg_id, src, dst, payload hash
Success indicator at destination

scenes.caption: Link 1→3 is set to 100% drop rate. A partition exists between Node 1 and Node 3 — no messages can cross this link. Node 1 can still reach Node 0 and Node 2. The gossip round from Node 0 reaches all nodes except Node 1, which remains isolated. The partition is visualized as a red dashed edge with a padlock icon.
visual_elements: Red dashed edge between Node1 and Node3 with padlock icon
Node1 shown in amber 'ISOLATED' state
Message paths shown as grey dashed lines for failed deliveries
Nodes 0, 2, 3 show green 'RECEIVED' state

scenes.caption: The same gossip round is replayed with seed=42. The drop decisions (which are drawn from the shared RNG) are identical to the first run: same messages are dropped at same hops. This demonstrates deterministic replay: with the same seed, the exact same message interleaving is reproduced. The UI shows two side-by-side runs with identical outcomes.
system_events: drop decisions identical to first run with seed=42
visual_elements: Two-panel layout: 'Run 1' and 'Run 2 (seed=42)'
Identical message delivery outcomes shown in both panels
RNG state shown in both: 'draw from rng(seed=42) → same bits'
Highlight: 'Reproducible: same seed = same message drop pattern'

checks: Ports are assigned sequentially from 32768, never reused within a test
Drop rate is per-hop: a message may be delivered on hop 1 but dropped on hop 2
Same seed produces identical drop decisions across replays
100% drop rate creates a permanent partition on that link only

visualizations.title: Buffer Pool: Zero-Copy Allocation, Pooled Return, and NUMA Locality
visualizations.learning_goal: Understand how pooled buffers are allocated, how they return to the pool on drop, and how NUMA locality affects allocation decisions.
visualizations.why_it_matters: Every network and storage operation uses IoBuf/IoBufMut. Understanding the buffer contract (read_at_buf reuses the caller-provided buffer, pooled buffers return to pool on drop) is prerequisite to reasoning about memory pressure and I/O performance.
visualizations.default_state: BufferPool with 4 slots of 4KB. Two PooledBuf allocated. NUMA node 0 has 2 buffers, node 1 has 0. Pool utilization: 2/4.

scenes.caption: A 4KB PooledBuf is allocated from the BufferPool. The buffer is aligned to a 4KB boundary (page-aligned for storage, cache-line-aligned for network). The pool slot is marked as 'in use'. Reference count on the buffer is 1. The allocation path: find size class → pop from ArrayQueue freelist → return aligned pointer. If no slot is available, a new AlignedBuffer is allocated from the OS heap.
visual_elements: Pool grid: 4 slots as rectangles, slot 0 filled (blue, labelled 'PooledBuf A: 4KB, aligned 4096, ref=1')
NUMA node diagram: node 0 (left), node 1 (right), slot 0 highlighted on node 0
Allocation arrow: 'alloc 4KB' pointing to slot 0
refcount badge: '1' on slot 0

scenes.caption: Buffer A's PooledBuf is dropped. The Drop impl for PooledBuf returns the buffer to the pool it came from, not a global free list. The pool slot is marked 'available' again. The buffer is NOT freed to the OS — it is reused on the next allocation. This is the key performance property: pooled allocations avoid repeated syscalls and NUMA traffic.
visual_elements: Slot 0 transitions from blue (in use) to green (available)
Return arrow: PooledBuf A → pool with 'return to pool (not OS free)' label
refcount badge: '0' on slot 0
OS memory unchanged (no syscall for deallocation)

scenes.caption: All 4 pool slots are full. A 5th 4KB allocation is requested. With no NUMA awareness, the pool allocates from any node (typically node 0). With NUMA awareness, the allocator can either fail the allocation or evict an LRU buffer from the local node. The visualization shows the pool in red 'FULL' state with the overflow allocation failing or triggering eviction.
system_events: Overflow: allocate from OS heap (not pooled)
visual_elements: All 4 slots in red 'FULL' state
5th allocation shown as amber arrow labelled 'overflow (non-pooled)'
Pool metrics: 'pool_size=4, allocated=4, overflow=1'
New buffer shown outside pool as separate element labelled 'non-pooled (OS heap)'

scenes.caption: An IoBuf backed by Bytes (copy-on-write) is created from a slice. The Bytes variant uses a reference-counted pointer to the underlying data — zero copy on clone. When written to, the copy-on-write triggers and a new allocation is made for the writer. The visualization contrasts PooledBuf (returns to pool on drop) vs Bytes-backed (standard refcount drop to global allocator).
system_events: write to IoBuf → COW copy triggered
visual_elements: Two-panel layout: 'PooledBuf (IoBuf)' vs 'Bytes-backed (IoBuf)'
Bytes panel: single buffer shared between two IoBuf instances with refcount=2
COW trigger: write arrow splits the shared buffer into two separate allocations
PooledBuf panel: buffer returns to pool on drop (no OS free)

checks: PooledBuf is returned to the pool on drop, not freed to the OS
NUMA-aware allocation queries sysinfo to determine local node before allocating
Bytes-backed IoBuf uses refcount (clone is zero-copy, write triggers COW copy)
Pool exhaustion results in non-pooled (heap) allocation, not an error

visualizations.title: Clock Advancement: Simulated Time vs. Virtualized Time
visualizations.learning_goal: Understand that the deterministic clock only advances when the executor calls advance_time() or when a sleeper fires — it is cooperative time, not wall-clock virtualization.
visualizations.why_it_matters: Time in the deterministic runtime is entirely under application control. A task that never calls sleep() or timeout() never advances the clock — time is frozen from the application's perspective. This is the key distinction from emulated time: the application must cooperate.
visualizations.default_state: Task A: sleep(10ms). Task B: sleep(5ms). Both spawned. Clock at t=0ms. Cycle=1ms. No cycles executed yet.

scenes.caption: Both tasks are spawned and immediately call sleep(). Task A registers an alarm at t=10ms. Task B registers an alarm at t=5ms. Both tasks yield (Poll::Pending). The ready queue is empty. The alarm priority queue (min-heap) shows B's alarm at the top (earliest deadline = 5ms), A's alarm below it.
visual_elements: Alarm priority queue visualized as inverted tree: B(5ms) at top (min), A(10ms) below
Ready queue: [] (empty, greyed out)
Clock: 0ms
Both task nodes: amber (sleeping) with alarm icon
Sleeper label: 'waiting for alarm'

scenes.caption: Manual mode: one executor cycle runs. The ready queue is empty (no wakers fired), so skip_idle_time engages: the clock jumps directly to the next alarm's deadline (5ms, B's alarm). The alarm fires, B's waker is called, and B is re-queued as ready. Time has jumped 5ms in one cycle — demonstrating skip_idle_time optimization.
visual_elements: Clock arrow: 0ms → 5ms (jumped 5ms in one cycle)
B transitions: amber (sleeping) → green (ready)
A remains amber (its deadline is 10ms, not yet reached)
skip_idle_time banner: 'No ready tasks — jumping to earliest alarm'
Alarm queue: [A(10ms)] after B's alarm is popped

scenes.caption: Same scenario, skip_idle_time disabled. The clock advances by only 1ms per cycle (the cycle duration). B's 5ms alarm requires 5 cycles to fire. Task B receives 5 polls before waking. This demonstrates the performance tradeoff: skip_idle_time makes simulations faster but loses fidelity for tight alarm deadlines.
visual_elements: 5 clock ticks shown: 0ms → 1ms → 2ms → 3ms → 4ms → 5ms
B woken indicator after 5 ticks
Comparison panel: 'skip_idle: 1 cycle' vs 'no skip_idle: 5 cycles'
Performance delta shown: 'skip_idle is 5x faster for this scenario'

checks: Clock only advances when the executor calls advance_time() or when an alarm fires
skip_idle_time only engages when the ready queue is empty (all tasks are sleeping)
Alarm priority queue uses reverse ordering (earliest deadline at top of min-heap)
cycle_duration controls how much time advances per cycle when skip_idle is NOT engaged

visualizations.title: Fault Injection Playground: Deterministic Failure with FaultyStorage
visualizations.learning_goal: Understand how FaultyStorage injects deterministic failures based on shared RNG, and how partial writes enable testing of crash recovery paths.
visualizations.why_it_matters: Ad-hoc mocking of storage failures is fragile and unreproducible. FaultyStorage provides a deterministic, seeded fault injection layer — the same seed always produces the same fault pattern, enabling reproducible testing of recovery code.
visualizations.default_state: FaultyStorage wrapping MemStorage. write_rate=10%, partial_write_rate=50%, read_rate=0%. Seed=42. No operations yet.

scenes.caption: Seed=42 with 10% write failure rate. The first write operation succeeds (the RNG draw for op 1 was above the 10% threshold). The blob is written and synced. The operation log shows the RNG draw and the decision: 'draw=0.73 > threshold=0.10 → SUCCESS'.
visual_elements: RNG draw display: '0.73 (threshold: 0.10)'
Outcome: green 'SUCCESS' pill
Operation log: chronological list of operations with outcomes
Blob state: written data shown as hex dump

scenes.caption: Seed=42 with 10% write failure rate. The second write operation fails (the RNG draw was 0.03 < 0.10). The error is propagated to the caller. The blob may be in a partially-written state. This is the key scenario: FaultyStorage does NOT guarantee atomicity — testing recovery from partial writes is the whole point.
visual_elements: RNG draw display: '0.03 (threshold: 0.10)'
Outcome: red 'FAULT INJECTED' pill
Error callout: 'FaultyStorage: write failed (seed=42, op=2)'
Blob state: amber 'PARTIAL WRITE — potentially corrupt' indicator

scenes.caption: Seed=1337 with 10% write rate and 50% partial write rate. The RNG first determines this is a failure (0.07 < 0.10), then determines it's a partial failure (0.55 > 0.50). Some bytes are written before the failure is injected. The blob is left in a corrupted state: partial data at the target offset. When reopened, Header::missing() or Header::from() will detect the corruption.
system_events: inner.write_at() called with truncated buffer → partial corruption
visual_elements: RNG draws: [0.07 (fail), 0.55 (partial)]
Outcome: red 'PARTIAL WRITE' pill with 'n bytes written before failure'
Blob hex dump: first N bytes written, remainder zeros or garbage
Recovery path highlighted: 'Blob reopen → Header::missing → automatic recovery'

scenes.caption: Same operations with same seed=42. The RNG draws are identical: [0.73, 0.03, ...]. The same write fails at the same operation index. This is the reproducibility guarantee: ship the seed alongside the test, and CI reproduces the exact fault pattern every time.
visual_elements: Two runs side-by-side: 'Run 1 (seed=42)' and 'Replay (seed=42)'
Identical RNG draw sequences shown
Identical failure points marked with red vertical lines aligned across both runs
Reproducibility badge: 'Seed 42: deterministic across runs'

checks: Same seed always produces identical failure pattern
partial_write_rate is applied to FAILED operations only (not all writes)
Partial write corrupts the blob by writing some bytes before failing
Corrupt blob is detected on reopen via Header validation

visualizations.title: Metric Scope Lifecycle: Registration, Encoding, and Automatic Deregistration
visualizations.learning_goal: Understand how scoped metrics are registered, how ScopeGuard ensures deregistration when a scope ends, and how MetricEncoder coalesces multiple registries into a deduplicated OpenMetrics output.
visualizations.why_it_matters: In long-running services, metrics from completed epochs/consensus-rounds must be cleaned up to prevent unbounded memory growth. The ScopeGuard ensures this cleanup happens automatically when a context scope is dropped.
visualizations.default_state: Root context with no metrics registered. Registry contains only the root scope. MetricEncoder output is empty.

scenes.caption: A counter 'requests_total' is registered on the root context with label 'type=read'. The metric is added to the root scope's prometheus registry. When MetricEncoder encodes the root registry, the metric appears in the output with the full OpenMetrics format: requests_total{kind="Counter", name="type=read"}.
system_events: metric added to root scope registry
visual_elements: Registry diagram: root scope contains 'requests_total'
OpenMetrics output panel: 'requests_total{kind="Counter", name="type=read"} 0'
Scope badge on metric: 'root'

scenes.caption: A scoped context 'epoch_1' is created via context.with_label('epoch_1'). A gauge 'epoch_height' is registered on this scoped context. The gauge appears in epoch_1's scoped registry, not the root registry. The metric label is prefixed with the scope: 'epoch_height{kind="Gauge", name="epoch_1/type=read"}'.
visual_elements: Two-level registry diagram: root scope (top), epoch_1 scope (nested below)
epoch_height metric in epoch_1 scope, NOT in root
Metric label: 'epoch_1/type=read' showing scope prefix
ScopeGuard shown as a 'gate' between epoch_1 and root

scenes.caption: All clones of the epoch_1 scoped context are dropped. The ScopeGuard's Drop impl runs: it removes the scoped registry from the root registry's BTreeMap<u64, PrometheusRegistry>. The epoch_1 metrics are no longer visible in the encoded output — they have been cleanly removed. This prevents metric accumulation across epochs.
visual_elements: epoch_1 scope gate closing (animated)
epoch_1 registry removed from BTreeMap
MetricEncoder output now shows ONLY root metrics (epoch_height gone)
Scope lifecycle badge: 'epoch_1: CLEANED UP'

scenes.caption: Attempting to register a metric with the same name and identical attributes on the same scope causes a panic (duplicate metric). The duplicate check is done in the prometheus registry. This enforces metric discipline: use with_attribute() to add dimensions rather than re-registering identical metrics.
visual_elements: First register: green 'registered' pill
Second register: red 'PANIC' pill with 'duplicate metric: requests_total{kind="Counter"}'
Stack trace excerpt shown
Correction callout: 'Use with_attribute() to add dimensions instead'

scenes.caption: Two scopes both register a counter 'events_total' with identical HELP and TYPE but different label dimensions (scope A vs scope B). MetricEncoder collects all families, groups by metric name (not by scope), and deduplicates the HELP/TYPE headers. The output shows a single events_total family with both scope variants as separate label sets.
visual_elements: Two scope registries each with 'events_total'
MetricEncoder merging diagram: two input families → one output family with two label sets
Output: 'events_total {scope="A"} 5\nevents_total {scope="B"} 3'
HELP string shown once (deduplicated), not twice

checks: Scoped metrics are prefixed with the context's label
ScopeGuard drop removes the scoped registry from root's BTreeMap
After scope drop, MetricEncoder output contains no metrics from that scope
Registering the same metric name with identical attributes on the same scope panics
